mechanism: Allocation of subjective value determined by whether agent relies on affective intensity cue (yielding step-function sensitivity to quantity) versus quantitative calculation (yielding proportional sensitivity). Preference formation mixes these modes; affective engagement draws attention to affect itself and away from scope, whereas calculation explicitly weights scope. Value emerges from interaction of emotional content intensity and attentional focus direction.

Prior exertion of executive control depletes a limited internal resource required for subsequent executive control; depletion magnitude depends on which specific executive function (attention control, response inhibition, updating working memory) was initially exerted; different functions impair different downstream functions (inhibition and updating share resource, but maintenance does not).

Temporal perspective biases mental simulation intensity: future-directed thinking (anticipation) engages more extensive episodic simulation than past-directed thinking (retrospection), generating stronger emotional arousal independent of event characteristics. This overgeneralization of the anticipation-simulation association to hypothetical events suggests processing differences (beyond just external uncertainty) drive the asymmetry.

Metacognitive difficulty cue (disfluency from degraded perceptual input or embodied effort signals) triggers System 2 analytical processing by serving as an alarm that simple intuitive judgment may be insufficient; this shifts reasoning from heuristic to systematic mode via increased cognitive effort allocation, not via slowing forced by stimulus properties.

Repetition effect on lexical access comprises two independent components: a brief, frequency-insensitive lexical activation lingering from recent processing, and a longer-term, frequency-sensitive episodic component reflecting memory traces that develop only when subjects make task-relevant decisions about primes. Frequency attenuation arises entirely from episodic involvement; pure lexical priming shows no frequency sensitivity.

Spacing effects in memory arise from two dissociable processes: in cued-memory tasks (recognition, frequency judgment), spacing improves performance via rehearsal strategy that allocates fewer rehearsals to massed repetitions perceived as 'already learned'; in free recall, spacing benefits arise from automatic encoding of contextual change information during study-phase retrieval of repeated items, independent of rehearsal.
